title: Refund and Reversal Spike Detection by Merchant

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'PSD2' ‚Üí 'Art.95'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

spl:
index=payments sourcetype="paygw:txn" earliest=-24h
| eval txn_tag="refund"
| eval eur_equiv=coalesce(amount_eur, amount*fx_rate)
| stats count as txns, dc(authorization_id) as auth_dc, sum(eval(eur_equiv>100000)) as very_high by merchant_id, txn_tag, bin(_time,1h)
| eval dup_suspect=if(txns>auth_dc AND txn_tag="dup", txns-auth_dc, 0)
| eval uc_id="22.22.22"
| where very_high>0 OR dup_suspect>5 OR txn_tag="refund"
| sort - very_high
| head 200
description: Provides transaction-level integrity and monitoring evidence aligned with PSD2 operational and security expectations for ASPSPs and payment processors.
value: Provides transaction-level integrity and monitoring evidence aligned with PSD2 operational and security expectations for ASPSPs and payment processors.
grandmaExplanation: We give you transaction-level integrity and monitoring evidence aligned with PSD2 operational and security expectations for ASPSPs and payment processors ‚Äî so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Normalize field extractions and CIM tags; (2) schedule hourly or near-real-time; (3) route alerts to SOC/compliance queues; (4) export evidence packs for supervisory review.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk DB Connect (2686).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=payments` `paygw:txn` ‚Äî authorization_id, merchant_id, amount_eur, fx_rate, reversal_flag.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
(1) Normalize field extractions and CIM tags; (2) schedule hourly or near-real-time; (3) route alerts to SOC/compliance queues; (4) export evidence packs for supervisory review.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=payments sourcetype="paygw:txn" earliest=-24h
| eval txn_tag="refund"
| eval eur_equiv=coalesce(amount_eur, amount*fx_rate)
| stats count as txns, dc(authorization_id) as auth_dc, sum(eval(eur_equiv>100000)) as very_high by merchant_id, txn_tag, bin(_time,1h)
| eval dup_suspect=if(txns>auth_dc AND txn_tag="dup", txns-auth_dc, 0)
| eval uc_id="22.22.22"
| where very_high>0 OR dup_suspect>5 OR txn_tag="refund"
| sort - very_high
| head 200
```

Understanding this SPL

**Refund and Reversal Spike Detection by Merchant (PSD2)** ‚Äî Provides transaction-level integrity and monitoring evidence aligned with PSD2 operational and security expectations for ASPSPs and payment processors.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=payments` `paygw:txn` ‚Äî authorization_id, merchant_id, amount_eur, fx_rate, reversal_flag. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk DB Connect (2686). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: payments; **sourcetype**: paygw:txn. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=payments, sourcetype="paygw:txn", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **txn_tag** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **eur_equiv** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by merchant_id, txn_tag, bin(_time,1h)** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **dup_suspect** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **uc_id** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where very_high>0 OR dup_suspect>5 OR txn_tag="refund"` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
‚Ä¢ Limits the number of rows with `head`.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Column chart (very_high), Table (merchant_id), Single value (dup_suspect)
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
